title: Terracotta slip wash on air-dry clay

glossaryTerms.definition: fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
clay diluted with water to a brushable or pourable consistency
the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join

content.text: A terracotta wash is a dilute, red-tinted
slip
or acrylic mix brushed over
bone-dry

air-dry clay to give it the warm, red-brown tone of fired earthenware. The thin liquid sinks into surface texture and dried crevices, darkening recesses and leaving raised areas lighter, which produces the tonal depth of real terracotta. A PVA coat over the dry wash protects it and adds a slight sheen.
When to use it

Apply the wash only after the clay has reached bone-dry : any remaining moisture will cause the wash to bead or lift. The technique suits textured surfaces (pinch-pot walls, impressed patterns, rope coils) because the tonal variation is most readable where the surface has relief. Smooth, flat pieces also accept the wash, but the result reads as a flat colour field rather than a tonal surface. Avoid applying to pieces that are still at the
leather-hard
stage: adding moisture at that point softens the clay and can cause deformation.
What you need
Step by step
Confirm the piece is bone-dry: it should feel room temperature all over, with no cool patches that signal remaining moisture. If in doubt, leave for another 12 hours.

Thin the red slip with water until it is the consistency of whole milk. It should flow off the brush and leave a translucent stain rather than an opaque coat. A small test on the base of the piece will show whether the colour reads correctly.
Brush the wash over the entire piece with the mop brush in overlapping strokes. Work around the form so no area dries before an adjacent section is covered. The clay will absorb the liquid quickly.

While the wash is still wet, use a barely damp sponge to wipe the high points of the surface lightly. This lifts colour from ridges and raised areas, leaving it concentrated in recesses. The contrast gives a fired-clay appearance. If the wash has already dried before you sponge, dampen the sponge slightly more and work gently : too much pressure will abrade the clay surface.
Leave to dry for at least two hours. A second wash coat deepens the tone. For a richer terracotta, apply two coats without sponging and sponge only after the second coat.

Once the wash is fully dry and the colour is even, brush on undiluted PVA with a clean mop brush. Cover all surfaces including the base. Leave to dry clear, about 30 minutes. A second PVA coat adds water resistance for indoor planters.
What it looks like when it works

The finished surface reads as a warm, even terracotta tone that is darker in crevices and lighter on raised ridges. There are no streaks, peel lines, or pale uncoated patches. The PVA seal sits clear across the whole surface with no cloudy bloom. The piece looks like unglazed earthenware at a distance.
Common mistakes
Wash applied while clay is still damp: the liquid softens the surface and the piece can crack or deform. Always confirm bone-dry before starting.
Wash too opaque: the result looks painted rather than aged. Thin the slip further and build colour with two or three light coats.
Sponging too aggressively: the clay surface abrades and crumbles, especially at thin edges. Use a barely damp sponge and a light touch : wipe rather than scrub.
PVA applied over wet wash: the two liquids mix and the PVA goes patchy and cloudy. The wash must be completely dry to the touch before sealing.
